regions: Nationwide — concentrated in urban areas. Northern cities: New York, Philadelphia, Boston, Chicago, Washington DC
Southern states: throughout, both urban and rural households

what_they_did.daily_work: Performed household labor in someone else's home — cooking, cleaning, laundry, childcare, and general maintenance. Live-in servants occupied a room in the employer's house and were on call at all hours. Day workers ('chars' or 'daily women') came in for specific hours. In the South, domestic work was overwhelmingly performed by Black women; in Northern cities, Irish and Scandinavian immigrant women dominated before 1900, followed by Black women during the Great Migration.
what_they_did.seasonal_cycle: Year-round employment. Spring and fall brought heavy cleaning cycles. Summer might bring travel with the employer's family or temporary unemployment when families left the city. Holiday seasons brought extra cooking and entertaining demands.
tools_and_equipment: Washboard and wringer (before electric washing machines)
Flat iron heated on the stove
Coal or wood stove for cooking and heating laundry water
what_they_did.typical_hours: Live-in servants: effectively on duty 14-16 hours per day, 6-7 days per week, with half a day off on Thursdays and Sundays (the 'maid's day off'). Day workers: 8-12 hours per day.

economics.typical_arrangement: Live-in servants received room, board, and a cash wage. Day workers received only cash. Wages were set entirely by the employer with no collective bargaining and no legal minimums until the 1970s (domestic workers were excluded from the 1938 Fair Labor Standards Act).
economics.annual_income_range: 1900-1910: $2-$5 per week ($104-$260/year) plus room and board for live-in servants. 1920s-1930s: $5-$15 per week. During the Depression, wages in some cities dropped to as low as $2-$3 per week. 1940s-1950s: $15-$35 per week ($780-$1,820/year). A 1920 Women's Bureau study reported median domestic worker earnings of $6.35 per week in Northern cities.
economics.comparison: Domestic work was among the lowest-paid occupations in America. A meatpacking worker or textile mill hand earned two to three times what a domestic servant earned. The non-cash component (room and board for live-in workers) made direct comparison difficult, but the economic disadvantage was stark.
economics.debt_cycle: Not a formal debt system, but the extreme dependency of live-in servants on employers created economic vulnerability. Dismissal meant simultaneous loss of income and housing. In the Depression-era 'slave markets' of the Bronx, Black women stood on street corners waiting for white housewives to hire them for a day's work at whatever price was offered.

hazards: Chemical exposure from cleaning agents (lye, ammonia, caustic soda)
Back injuries from heavy lifting (laundry, furniture moving)
Repetitive strain from scrubbing and wringing
Sexual harassment and assault by male members of the employer's household — widely documented but rarely prosecuted
working_conditions.living_conditions: Live-in servants occupied the smallest, least desirable rooms in the house — attic rooms, basement quarters, or back-of-kitchen alcoves. Privacy was minimal. Day workers lived in their own homes, typically in overcrowded urban neighborhoods. In the South, domestic workers often walked long distances from Black neighborhoods to white employers' homes.
working_conditions.family_involvement: Domestic work created agonizing family separations. Live-in servants left their own children with relatives to care for employers' children. Day workers left home before dawn and returned after dark. Daughters frequently followed mothers into domestic service — it was one of the few occupations consistently open to Black women.
working_conditions.autonomy: Highly variable — depended entirely on the individual employer. Some domestic workers exercised considerable authority in running a household. Others were micromanaged and demeaned. The fundamental power imbalance — one worker, one employer, in a private home with no witnesses — made domestic service uniquely vulnerable to exploitation.

push_factors: World War II opened factory and clerical jobs to women previously confined to domestic work
Civil rights movement challenged the racial caste system underlying Southern domestic service
Increasing education and opportunity for Black women after 1950
Social Security coverage extended to domestics in 1950, but enforcement was weak, making long-term security uncertain
why_they_left.what_replaced_them: Commercial cleaning services, day care centers, restaurants and prepared food, washing machines and other household appliances, and the entry of middle-class white women into the workforce (which, paradoxically, both reduced demand for some domestic services and increased demand for childcare). Immigrant women from Latin America, the Caribbean, and Asia filled the remaining domestic positions from the 1970s onward.
why_they_left.narrative_hook: The work that {ancestor_name} did in someone else's kitchen in {county} was the kind of labor that rarely made it into the record books — but it put food on the table, kept children in school, and held families together through decades when other doors were closed.

records_generated.what_it_shows: Name, age, race, marital status, and birthplace of domestic servants enumerated in the employer's household
records_generated.where_to_find: Federal census records on Ancestry.com, FamilySearch
records_generated.research_tip: Live-in domestic servants are enumerated in the employer's household, not their own. Search for an ancestor by name across all households in a city — they may appear as a 'servant' or 'domestic' in a household with a different surname.

records_generated.record_type: Employment agency records and domestic worker registries
records_generated.what_it_shows: Names, previous positions, skills, references, wages requested
records_generated.where_to_find: Scattered in city archives and historical societies. The YWCA operated placement services for domestic workers in many cities; their records are in YWCA archives and local historical societies.
records_generated.research_tip: Employment agency records survive irregularly but can provide detailed personal information including character references and employment history

records_generated.what_it_shows: Full name, date of birth, parents' names, employer name and address at time of application
records_generated.where_to_find: Social Security Administration (for deceased individuals), Ancestry.com has digitized many
records_generated.research_tip: When domestic workers first became eligible for Social Security coverage in 1950, many filed SS-5 applications that listed their employer — providing a direct link between servant and household

census_clues.what_to_look_for: A woman listed as 'Servant' or 'Domestic' in a household where the other members share a different surname. In the South, look for Black women listed in white households. For day workers, look for women listed as 'Laundress' or 'Cook' in their own households with no employer named.
census_clues.red_flags: A Black woman listed as a 'servant' in a white household in the 1870s-1880s South may have been working for the same family (or their descendants) who enslaved her before emancipation. Check the slaveholder schedules of 1850 and 1860.

cultural_context: Domestic service was the occupation most intimately connected to the racial and gender hierarchies of American life. For Black women in the South, it was often the only paid work available — a continuation of the labor relationships of slavery in a new economic form. The intimacy of the work (caring for white children, handling the family's private belongings) created complex emotional dynamics that writers from Langston Hughes to Kathryn Stockett have explored, though the power imbalance was never in doubt. The domestic worker's struggle for dignity, fair wages, and legal protection continues to this day.

counter_narratives.claim: Domestic service was not exclusively a Black woman's occupation
counter_narratives.context: In Northern cities before the Great Migration, Irish, German, and Scandinavian immigrant women dominated domestic service. In the West, Chinese men worked as domestic servants in the late 19th century. In the Southwest, Mexican American women were heavily represented. The racial composition of the domestic workforce varied by region and era.

counter_narratives.claim: Some domestic workers wielded significant household authority
counter_narratives.context: Long-serving cooks and housekeepers in wealthy households often managed household budgets, supervised other servants, and exercised real authority in the domestic sphere. Their skills were valued and their tenure could span decades. This did not eliminate the fundamental power imbalance, but it complicates the narrative of pure exploitation.

source_references.author: Clark-Lewis, Elizabeth
source_references.title: Living In, Living Out: African American Domestics in Washington, D.C., 1910-1940
source_references.notes: Oral history-based study of the transition from live-in to day work among Black domestic workers — essential for understanding the daily experience

source_references.author: Katzman, David M.
source_references.title: Seven Days a Week: Women and Domestic Service in Industrializing America
source_references.notes: Comprehensive national survey of domestic service from the Civil War through the early 20th century

source_references.author: Hunter, Tera W.
source_references.title: To 'Joy My Freedom: Southern Black Women's Lives and Labors After the Civil War
source_references.notes: Examines Black women's domestic work and resistance strategies in Atlanta — including the remarkable 1881 washerwomen's strike
